what_it_does: Defines the contract and integration surface for how a room (a full-viewport panel) plugs into the house: registration API, lifecycle hooks, shared context, mandatory design laws, ground modes, and construction style.
observations: The house is index.html; the Gallery is room 1 and remains the default view; rooms 2–4 (Bench, Map, Almanac) are full-viewport panels that slide over the gallery on a shared canvas.
Navigation, transitions, the room bar, lazy mounting, and hash routing (#room=bench) are owned by surface/rooms/shell.js; shared room chrome is owned by surface/rooms/rooms.css.
Each room is a single self-contained file surface/rooms/<id>.js that calls DNA.rooms.register with id, name, icon, ground, order, mount, enter, and leave.
The ctx object passed to mount/enter/leave exposes live tokens, warmth, manifest, DNA.api, navigate, DNA.org organisms, and an HTML escaper.

Mandatory laws include registry-driven styling via CSS/address variables, data only from ctx.api.dna and ctx.manifest, signal economy (one gold focal), provision of zones/diagrams, motion laws, voice rules, responsiveness, and specified fonts.
Grounds are first-class: instrument is a dark drafting-table world; paper is the gallery's warm paper world.
Construction style is vanilla JS plus DOM strings, no frameworks, no build step, with CSS classes namespaced by room id or using shared classes from rooms.css.
standouts: A prescriptive 'laws a room MUST obey' section intended to be checked by reviewers.
The ctx object centralizes all data access and design-organism generation, enforcing a single integration surface.
Signal-economy rule: one gold focal action per view, with gold treated as punctuation rather than fill.
Grounds as meaningful aesthetic modes (instrument vs paper) tied to an archived studio metaphor.
Strict standalone construction: rooms may not read each other and must use only the provided context and design tokens.
conventions: Follows a design-system contract pattern by referencing canonical files (dna/CANON.md, dna/MOTION.md, dna/SPACES.md, dna/voice.json).
Uses CSS custom properties and an address vocabulary for all visual values.
Enforces vanilla-JS, DOM-string rendering and per-room CSS class namespacing.
Breaks the typical component-framework model by requiring each room to be a standalone file with no inter-room dependencies.
concerns: The 'reviewers check these' clause implies external enforcement; the file itself contains no verification mechanism.
Some context methods (tok, warmth, manifest) are described as live but the update lifecycle is not specified.
The prohibition on 'invented hex' and 'magic registries' is a human-review rule, not an automatic guard.
The requirement to support both grounds and both sizes without examples may be difficult to validate from this file alone.
notes: This is the plug-in contract every room must satisfy to join the house surface, covering lifecycle, shared context, design laws, and construction rules.
questions: Where and how is DNA.rooms.register implemented, and how does the registry enforce uniqueness or ordering?
What are the exact semantics of mount versus enter/leave regarding DOM preservation and state?
How does shell.js parse hash routes and coordinate transitions between rooms?
What is the complete set of DNA.org organisms and icon names available to rooms?
How are the CSS variables and address vocabulary kept in sync with the dna canonical files?
purpose_vs_actual: matches — the file states it defines how a room plugs into the house, and it does so through registration, lifecycle, context, laws, grounds, and style.
intention_signals: It is trying to create a disciplined, reviewable plugin architecture so that every new room integrates consistently with the house's data, design language, and motion laws while remaining self-contained.
novelty: The explicit 'laws' reviewed by reviewers, the context object bundling data faces and design organisms, and the first-class 'grounds' metaphor (instrument drafting-table vs paper gallery) make the contract unusually prescriptive and aesthetic.
contribution: It contributes the integration contract and quality gates a fused room system would need to keep: the registration API, lifecycle hooks, shared context, mandatory design-law checklist, ground-mode distinction, and standalone vanilla-JS construction rules.
summary_for_embedding: CONTRACT.md defines the registration API, lifecycle hooks, shared context, mandatory design laws, responsive and ground requirements, and vanilla-JS construction style for rooms that plug into the house surface.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to make each room a self-contained, reviewable plugin that conforms to the shared design system and data-access patterns.
